title: Christology and Eschatology
speaker: Mr. Isaiah Couch
source_files: 05-07-23 - Adult Sumday School -Christology and Eschatlogy - Mr. Isaiah Couch.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Importance of Christology
subpoints: Continuing study of the Belgic Confession from a previous lesson
R.C. Sproul: the church's greatest weakness is its Christology
If we get Christ wrong, we get the gospel wrong
Machen: liberals say 'Jesus is God' but mean it with a desperately low view of God

outline.title: Article 19: The Two Natures of Christ (Belgic Confession)
subpoints: The divine and human natures are inseparably united in one person
No confusion, no change, no division, no separation — the Chalcedonian definition
Historical errors: Anabaptists denied the full humanity of Christ; Servetus denied his full divinity
The church councils: Nicaea (325), Constantinople (381), Ephesus (431), Chalcedon (451)
The Heidelberg Catechism Q&A 16-17: why must the mediator be truly man and truly God?

outline.title: The Link Between Christology and Eschatology
subpoints: Belgic Confession Article 19: our salvation and resurrection depend on the reality of Christ's body
Paul addresses deniers of the resurrection in 1 Corinthians 15
If Christ is not physically risen, our faith is futile and we are still in our sins
But now Christ IS risen — the first fruits of those who have fallen asleep

outline.title: Article 37: The Last Judgment (Belgic Confession)
subpoints: Christ will come visibly and bodily from heaven to judge the living and the dead
All the dead will be raised and their souls reunited with their proper bodies
The judgment is terrible for the wicked and desirable for the elect
Full deliverance perfected; tears wiped away; glory beyond imagination
Debrae wrote from prison expecting his martyrdom, yet looked to the resurrection with hope

outline.title: Practical Comfort: The Resurrection and Death
subpoints: The intermediate state (soul with Christ) is far better but not yet complete — we were made body and soul
We look forward to the reunion of glorified body and soul
Personal testimony: the death of a family member understood through a proper eschatology
The Heidelberg Catechism: with body and soul, in life and in death, I belong to my faithful Savior Jesus Christ

summary:
Mr. Isaiah Couch continues his study of the Belgic Confession in an adult Sunday School lesson, examining two articles that have been personally formative in his own theological journey. The lesson addresses Article 19 on the two natures of Christ and Article 37 on the last judgment, showing how a proper Christology grounds a proper eschatology.

On Christology, Couch traces the church's historic confession that Jesus is truly God and truly man — two natures united in one person without confusion, change, division, or separation, as defined by the Council of Chalcedon in 451. He engages the historical heresies of the Anabaptists (who denied Christ's true humanity) and Servetus (who denied his true divinity), showing that Debrae in the Belgic Confession stands with the full weight of church history at Nicaea, Constantinople, Ephesus, and Chalcedon. The Heidelberg Catechism's Q&A 16–17 provides the practical answer: Jesus must be truly man to pay for sin in human nature, and truly God to endure the infinite wrath of God and give worth and efficacy to his obedience.

The transition to eschatology is made through a single line in Article 19: "our salvation and resurrection depend also on the reality of his body." This leads into Article 37, the Belgic's article on the last judgment, which Couch reads in full. He emphasizes that all the dead will be raised and their souls reunited with their proper bodies — a truth that is both comfort to the elect and warning to the unbeliever. Couch closes with a personal testimony about the unexpected death of his father-in-law, noting that a proper understanding of the bodily resurrection provided a peace and comfort he had never before experienced in bereavement.
transcript:
## Introduction

For the adult class today, Isaiah is going to be teaching and he's going to solve every controversy the church has ever had. We've been waiting for millennia for this, so no pressure Isaiah that. We're going to rent. San Remo. San Spectacular. How did you be willing to pass these up? Thank you very much. Well look good morning everyone, once again. It's interesting that Nick mentions the millennium — I'll be talking about eschatology a little bit, so that's always fun. But thank you, once again, to the session and asking me to speak, once again, a great honor.

I also want to just take a quick second to thank everybody that helped out in gifting me and my family the four volume set from Reformation Heritage books on the different confessions and catechisms of the church in the 16th and 17th century. It's a beautiful addition to my library and I'm already using it and so thank you everybody that wasn't involved in gifting that to us.

In my previous lesson, I introduced us to the Belgic Confession, the confession of faith from the 16th century and the substance that is contained in a few of its articles. We looked at its author, Guido de Bres, the thematic arc of the confession itself and then examined two articles on the recovery of fallen man and our justification as sinners through Jesus Christ. And this morning, I want to continue to examine this confession. Take up and read two more articles contained within and my prayer again as last time was that this would encourage us to be a people that remember and know the substance of our confessions that declare the doctrine that we say together we believe.

As de Bres said before being led to his death, "My brothers, I am condemned to death today for the doctrine of the Son of God, praise be to him. I would never have thought that God would have given me such an honor." In preparing the lesson, I thought if de Bres could praise God for the great honor to die for the doctrine of the Son of God, how much more should we praise our God for giving us the honor to live and to study this same doctrine?

## Article 19: The Two Natures of Christ

So the first article we're going to address, very special to me now in my own heart, is Article 19 on the union and distinction of the two natures in the person of Christ. The Belgic Confession says this:

"We believe that by being thus conceived, the person of the Son has been inseparably united and joined together with human nature. In such a way that there are not two sons of God nor two persons but two natures united in a single person with each nature retaining its own distinct properties. Thus, his divine nature has always remained uncreated without beginning of days or end of life, filling heaven and earth, his human nature has not lost its properties but continues to have those of a creature. It has a beginning of days, it has a finite nature and retains all that belongs to a real body. And even though he by his resurrection gave it immortality, that nonetheless did not change the reality of his human nature for our salvation and resurrection depend also on the reality of his body. But these two natures are so united together in one person that they are not even separated by his death. These are the reasons why we confess him to be very God and very man, very God in order to conquer death by his power and very man that he might die for us in the weakness of his flesh."

When I really began to study the Belgic Confession, I wanted to see if it stood alone in church history. And what I mean by that is, since de Bres was the sole author, I had heard some say that, well, since there's no ecumenical counsel or there's no group of people, it's dangerous to have just the interpretation of one person. But when I began to study the confession, I realized that what de Bres was speaking about was nothing new in substance and doctrine to the church.

When speaking on Christology, the doctrine of Christ, I once heard R.C. Sproul say that the church's greatest weakness is not its soteriology, the doctrine of salvation, not its anthropology, the doctrine of man, or even its eschatology, the doctrine of the end. But its greatest weakness is its Christology, the doctrine of Christ. And the reason Sproul said this is because he was stressing the importance that if the church does not understand the doctrine of Christ, it does not understand the gospel.

We saw the critical importance of a proper Christology at the table lectures, in how Machen a hundred years ago was teaching on the vital importance of this doctrine. Machen says in Christianity and Liberalism, "The liberal preacher is often ready to speak of the deity of Christ. He is often ready to say, Jesus is God, but unfortunately, language is valuable only as the expression of thought. The English word God has no particular virtue in itself. Its importance depends altogether on the meaning which is attached to it. When therefore the liberal preacher says, Jesus is God, the significance of the utterance depends altogether on what is meant by God. The modern liberal says that Jesus is God, not because they think highly of Jesus, but because they think desperately low of God."

Sproul, Machen, and de Bres are just a few examples of men who saw the importance of the church having a proper Christology. To change an analogy that Calvin and Luther used, one might say that a proper Christology is the hinge upon which the door of the gospel hangs. If we get Christ wrong, we get the gospel wrong.

The incarnation of Christ is far too often a phrase that we have become all too familiar with, and it seems to have lost most of its weight. Daniel R. Hyde reminds us in his exposition of the Belgic Confession that the incarnation is no mere phrase. It was a climactic, redemptive event. At that moment, heaven and earth fell into a hushed silence as God did something he had never done before.

In the previous article, Article 18, de Bres writes on the incarnation and he stresses the importance of the Son of God taking unto himself a human nature soul and body. The eternal Son who took upon him the form of a servant and became like unto man, really assumed the true human nature with all its infirmities sin excluded. And did not only assume human nature as to the body, but also a true human soul that he might be a real man. For since the soul was lost as well as the body, it was necessary that he should take both upon himself to save both.

So we confess, in opposition to the heresy of the Anabaptists, who denied that Christ assumed human flesh of his mother, that Christ partook of the flesh and blood of the children, so that in truth, he is our Emmanuel, that is to say, God with us. As we talked a little bit about the errors of the Anabaptists in the previous lesson — I just want to stress again that Baptists today are not Anabaptists. De Bres was addressing the Anabaptists due to the revival of old errors.

One of the leaders of the Anabaptist movement presented that since the first Adam was totally sinful after the fall, he could never again bear any good fruit. His argument was that Jesus was born in Mary, but not of the same substance as Mary. His reasoning was that if Jesus were of her substance, he himself would be sinful. So what you end up with in this Anabaptist belief is that the person of Jesus does not truly possess a human body made of the same substance as us, which means that he also does not truly have a human soul or nature. So you are left with someone who is truly God, but not truly man.

Another example at this time was held by a man named Michael Servetus. Servetus claimed that Jesus was truly human in a miraculous way, but he was not to be acknowledged as truly God. He said that Jesus only possessed the title of God by the extraordinary calling, majesty, and authority that was given to him by the Father. So you are left with someone who is truly man, but not truly God.

And de Bres shows in this article that he stands with the early church councils at Nicaea, Constantinople, Ephesus, and Chalcedon, in confessing that our Lord Jesus Christ is the God-man, truly God truly man who possesses both a divine nature and a human nature.

At the Council of Nicaea in 325, the very substance of Jesus was debated. Was Jesus Homoousios — same substance — or Homoiousios — similar substance — with the Father, or was he Heteroousios — different substance from the Father? What comes out of this council at Nicaea is the Nicene Creed, which declares that Jesus is God of God, light of light, very God of very God, begotten, not made, being of one substance with the Father.

The councils at Constantinople in 381 and Ephesus in 431 continue to address errors with regard to the doctrine of Christ. Then at the Council of Chalcedon in 451, the church carefully established boundaries that we should not travel beyond. R.C. Sproul said it really well in his book Truths We Confess: "If we move away from Chalcedon in either direction, exaggerating either the divinity or the humanity of Christ, at the expense of the other, we will fall into heresy." The church used a method of definition called the way of negation — the Chalcedonian definition declares that the two natures of Christ undergo no confusion, no change, no division, and no separation.

The Heidelberg Catechism greatly helped me in answering my question as to why this is important. In question and answer 16 and 17, the Heidelberg says this:

Question 16: Why must the mediator be a true and righteous man? Answer: Because God's justice requires that human nature, which has sinned, must pay for its sins, but a sinner could never pay for others.

Question 17: Why must he also be true God? Answer: So that by the power of his divinity, he might bearing his humanity the weight of God's wrath and earn for us and restore to us, righteousness and life.

If Jesus is not truly man, he could not be our Savior — the one who was, as Galatians 4:4-4 says, born of a woman, born under the law, to redeem those who were under the law. And if Jesus is not truly God, he could not be able to endure the wrath of God and overcome death for us. This is why the Belgic Confession says: "These are the reasons we confess him to be very God and very man. Very God in order to conquer death by his power, very man that he might die for us in the weakness of his flesh."

## The Connection to Eschatology

In transitioning to our next article on the doctrine of the last day, eschatology — I know some of you might be thinking, how is he going to do that? How are you going to transition from Christology to eschatology? There is one line in this article that connected these two doctrines for me. De Bres writes in Article 19 that our salvation and resurrection depend also on the reality of his body.

When studying this article and leading into this last article, this portion of Article 19 immediately reminded me of when Paul addressed those who said that there is no resurrection of the dead. If you want, please turn with me to 1 Corinthians 15. 1 Corinthians 15, verses 12 through 17:

"Now if Christ is preached that he has been raised from the dead, how do some among you say that there is no resurrection of the dead? But if there is no resurrection of the dead then Christ is not risen. If Christ is not risen then our preaching is empty and your faith is also empty. Yes and we are found false witnesses of God because we have testified of God that he raised up Christ when he did not raise up, if, in fact, the dead do not rise. For if the dead do not rise, then Christ is not risen and if Christ is not risen, your faith is futile and you are still in your sins."

If Paul ended this passage here, it is truly a hopeless place for a believer. But praise be to God for verse 20: "But now Christ is risen from the dead." We do not confess a Christ that was only spiritually raised from the dead as the liberals say. We confess that our everlasting head who once was dead, who is truly God and truly man, was raised physically because as the confession says, our salvation and resurrection depend also on the reality of his body.

## Article 37: The Last Judgment

We now turn to the final article of the Belgic Confession and the hope and comfort that every believer truly has in the doctrine of the end. It is a longer article but I do want to read it in its entirety:

"Finally, we believe according to the word of God when the time appointed by the Lord, which is unknown to all creatures, is come and the number of the elect complete, then our Lord Jesus Christ will come from heaven corporally and visibly as He ascended with great glory and majesty to declare Himself judge of the living and the dead burning this old world with fire and flame to cleanse it. Then all men will personally appear before this great judge, both men and women and children that have been from the beginning of the world to the end thereof, being summoned by the voice of the archangel and by the sound of the trump of God. All the dead will be raised out of the earth and their souls will be joined and united with their proper bodies in which they formally lived. As for those who will then be living they will not die as the others but be changed in the twinkling of an eye and from corruptible become incorruptible. Then the books, that is to say the consciences, will be opened and the dead judged according to what they will have done in this world whether it be good or evil. Nay all men will give account of every idle word that they have spoken which the world only counts amusement and jest, and then the secrets and hypocrisy of men will be disclosed and laid open before all, and therefore the consideration of this judgment is justly terrible and dreadful to the wicked and ungodly but most desirable and comfortable to the righteous and elect because then their full deliverance will be perfected and there they will receive the fruits of their labor and trouble which they have borne. Their innocence will be known to all and they will see the terrible vengeance which God will execute on the wicked who most truly persecuted, oppressed and tormented them in this world and who shall be convicted by the testimony of their own consciences and who will become immortal but only to be tormented in the eternal fire which is prepared for the devil and his angels. But on the contrary the faithful and elect will be crowned with glory and honor and the son of God will confess their names before God his father and his elect angels. All tears will be wiped away from their eyes and their cause which is now condemned by many judges and magistrates as heretical and impious will then be known to be the cause of the son of God and for a gracious reward the Lord will cause them to possess such a glory as never entered into the heart of man to conceive. Therefore we expect that great day with most ardent desire to the end that we may fully enjoy the promises of God in Christ Jesus our Lord. Amen."

When speaking on the doctrine of eschatology, most discussions and debate center around the millennium. But de Bres focuses on the beautiful and glorious truth that unites all three millennial views — he says simply that our Lord Jesus Christ will come from heaven corporally and visibly to declare himself judge of the living and the dead. All the dead will be raised out of the earth and their souls will be joined and united with their proper bodies.

## Practical Comfort

In continuing to study the doctrine of eschatology, I have come to rejoice that while the intermediate state for the believer is far better than this life, we must remember that even that state is not natural and complete. We were made with body and soul. The soul that is presently with our risen Lord is there without a physical body, and that is not natural. And so we look to that day, not only for ourselves when we die, but also for all those who have fallen asleep in Christ, because it is on that day that their souls will be joined and united with their proper bodies in which they formally lived.

As many of you know, roughly six months ago, Becky's father died from a severe heart attack at their house down in Oregon. It was sudden and unexpected for our family. We were planning on going down to their place, have Thanksgiving with them. And this moment shook our family. And while we were grieving his death, I remember thinking to myself that something about his death was different from death that I had experienced in the past. And while I had experienced the death of loved ones before, what was different this time, I believe, was a proper understanding of the resurrection. There was a peace and comfort I had never experienced before. There was, as the confession says, a most ardent desire in looking forward to that glorious day because it is there that we may fully enjoy the promises of God in Christ Jesus our Lord.

In the final few months of his life, de Bres wrote several letters to his congregation, his mother, and his beloved wife, exhorting them to not grieve greatly over his coming death. De Bres encouraged them to look to the resurrection of the dead. He reminded them that death in this life is not the end. This understanding is something that I have truly come to appreciate and treasure in knowing that as the Heidelberg says, with body and soul in life and in death, I belong to my faithful Savior Jesus Christ.

In returning to our passage in 1 Corinthians 15, go down to verse 52:

"For the trumpet will sound and the dead will be raised incorruptible and we shall be changed. For this corruptible must put on incorruption and this mortal must put on immortality. So when this corruptible has put on incorruption and this mortal has put on immortality, then shall be brought to pass the saying that is written, Death is swallowed up in victory. O death, where is your sting? O Hades, where is your victory? The sting of death is sin and the strength of sin is the law, but thanks be to God who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ. Therefore my beloved brethren, be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that your labor is not in vain in the Lord."

## Conclusion

So in closing, I hope that we have seen how a proper Christology — understanding that Christ is truly God and truly man, was raised physically from the dead — leads us to a proper eschatology. We recognize that our salvation and resurrection depend on the reality of His body. Since Jesus is risen from the dead and has become the first fruits of those that have fallen asleep, when we are faced with death in our own lives, we will be comforted in fixing our eyes upon him, who will one day return to judge the living and the dead. And as our Bridegroom, he will gather His bride the church to Himself, where we will worship Him in spirit and in truth, in body and soul forevermore.

And to hear these words from Song of Solomon 2 of Christ gathering His church to Himself physically: "Rise up my love, my fair one and come away. For lo the winter is past the rain is over and gone. The flowers appear on the earth, the time of singing has come, and the voice of the turtle dove has been heard in our land, the fig tree puts forth her green figs, and the vines with tender grapes give a good smell. Rise up my love, my fair one, and come away."

Let us pray. Our Almighty God of comfort and peace. We thank you for our Lord Jesus Christ. Who before all the worlds as Nicene says was very God, was God of God light of light, being of one substance with you. And in the incarnation He took to Himself, the human body, the human nature, and the soul, without undergoing confusion, change, division, or separation. And as our Savior, He lived a perfect life of obedience, died in our place as the perfect sacrifice, was raised physically from the dead, is seated at your right hand, making intercession for us, and will one day come again to gather your church, take her to yourself, where we will enjoy you forever more. And so may we ever live and die in knowing this blessed comfort of both the doctrine of Christ and the doctrine of the last day. May your spirit cause all of us to grow in these areas. We ask all these things in the name of your Son by the power of your spirit to all to the praise of your glorious grace. Amen.
